_skill.name: graph_analysis
_skill.description: Per-graph description instructions for the VLM (brake, speed, throttle, speed_delta, trajectory_detailed, trajectory_gas_brake, trajectory_offset, trajectory_balance, push_limit, time_delta). Each entry tells the VLM how to read the graph precisely — what axes mean, what colours encode, what shapes/predicates to call out. Also carries cross-graph guidelines (triggered when specific graph combinations are present) and a trajectory shape vocabulary.
when_to_use: Injecting graph-specific checklists into the describe_graphs VLM prompt.
Looking up axis/colour conventions for a single graph id.
Resolving the cross-graph guidelines that apply to a graph combination.

throttle.title: Throttle Application: Expert vs Player

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)

visual_elements: Two overlapping line traces — expert_optimal_throttle (expert) and Physics_gas (player)
Legend identifies which colour is expert vs player
Grid lines for reading approximate values
throttle.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Read the legend — assign each colour to expert or player.  Note the
   left and right edge values for both traces.

2.Describe events (throttle application, throttle release, modulation dip) for both expert and player.
  For EVERY event mentioned, record the index range (start–end) where it
  occurs — never describe a feature with only a duration ("across X indices") 
  and no anchor. Make comparsions between expert and player for each event found.
    - Is the application/release "abrupt" or "gradual"?
    - Does the release show "trailing off" or an "abrupt drop"?
    - Is there any "modulation dip" (dip-and-recover)?

3. Enumerate ONLY clearly-visible events (throttle application, throttle
   release, modulation dip).  Do not invent events to satisfy a
   template — if a trace has one application and one release, report
   one and one.  Skip any event you cannot clearly distinguish from
   the grid.  For every event record the index on each trace.

4. Classify the segment using exactly one segment-class glossary term
   (flat-full segment, flat-zero segment, release-only segment,
   apply-only segment, release-and-reapply segment, multi-cycle
   segment, unmatch segment).

5. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide.  Lead with the shape difference 
  (release and application character,  modulation dips) anchored to 
  index ranges; then the matched events with the index on each trace; then the
  segment class callout.

glossary.flat-full segment: Both traces remain at full throttle (1.0) across the segment; no release or re-application is visible.
glossary.flat-zero segment: Both traces remain near zero across the segment; the segment is a brake or coast zone rather than a throttle phase.
glossary.release-only segment: A throttle release is visible but the segment ends before any re-application.
glossary.apply-only segment: A throttle application is visible but no prior release falls inside the window (segment starts mid-corner or after a coast).
glossary.release-and-reapply segment: A full release → near-zero hold → re-application cycle is visible within the segment (the typical case).
glossary.multi-cycle segment: More than one release / re-apply pair is visible (e.g. chicane or a series of esses) in travel order.
glossary.unmatch segment: One trace stays flat while the other is active, or the two traces do not share the same overall shape.
glossary.throttle release: Throttle drops from a consistent value and rests at near zero, with the negative slope potentially containing some fluctuation. The release modulation dip may occur during the release, but the overall slope should be negative. When compare a subject to a object, a subject has more abrupt releases if the length of the drop is shorter; more gradual releases if the drop is longer.
glossary.throttle application: Throttle rises from a consistent value and rests at the local maximum, with the positive slope potentially containing some fluctuation. The throttle modulation dip may occur during the throttle application, the overall slope should be positive. When compare a subject to an object, a subject has more abrupt applications if the length of the rise is shorter; more gradual applications if the drop is longer.
glossary.peak throttle: The maximum y-value the throttle trace reaches across the segment, with the index (or index range, if held).
glossary.release onset: start of throttle release. An index where the throttle trace first drops from a consistent value with a negative slope. A subject has an earlier release onset if this index is less than the other trace's release onset; later if it is greater.
glossary.application onset: start of throttle application. An index where the throttle trace begin rises from near zero with a positive slope. A subject has an earlier application onset if this index is less than the other trace's application onset; later if it is greater.
glossary.modulation dip: Occcur in mid-release down slope or mid-application raise slope where  the trace dips and recovers without returning to zero. when modulation  dip occurs for both traces at about the same index, compare the depth and  duration of the dips to determine whether the subject shows a more pronounced dip.

throttle.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph.  Every event MUST be anchored to an index range or specific index — never quote a duration alone.
(a) Detailed events — emit ONLY the clauses your chosen class
    requires.  Use whichever apply:

  - event comparison (release, application, release onset, application onset):
    "<event>: <Player/expert> starts at index A and ends at index B(if there is one); <Player/expert> 
    starts at index C and ends at index D(if there is one) — <Player/expert> is <earlier|later> 
    by N indices, or <more abrupt|more gradual> by M indices."

  - Modulation dip occurs within the release or application slope itself. When present
    on either trace, include as a separate clause:
      "<Player/expert>'s <release|application> across indices X–Y contains
      a modulation dip around index N (val); <other>'s <release|application>
      across the same range is monotonic."
    If both traces dip near the same index, compare depth and duration:
      "Both <release|application> slopes contain a modulation dip near
      index N; <Player/expert>'s dip is <deeper|shallower> by Δ and
      <longer|shorter> by D indices."

(b) Segment class callout (when applicable):
  "The segment is a <segment-class> — both traces <description of
   shape features that define the class, anchored to index ranges>."
common_description_errors: Verify expert vs player from the legend before describing
Refer to each trace as "player" or "expert" in the output — NEVER by its colour (no "the blue trace", "the red line", etc.).  Colour is only an internal cue for reading the legend
Do NOT invent events to fill the format. If the trace has only one application and one release, report only one of each
Anchor EVERY shape feature and event to an index range or specific index — "gradual across 5 indices" without a start index is insufficient; write "gradual across indices 94–99" instead
Suspect-pattern check: if your output shows the player consistently offset by exactly the same number of indices at every event, you are pattern-matching rather than reading — restart from step 3

brake.title: Brake Application: Expert vs Player

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)

visual_elements: Two overlapping line traces — expert_optimal_brake (expert) and Physics_brake (player)
Legend identifies which colour is expert vs player
Grid lines for reading approximate values
brake.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Read the legend — assign each colour to expert or player.  Note the
   left and right edge values for both traces.

2. Describe events (brake initiation, brake release, peak brake pressure, modulation dip) for both expert and player.
  For EVERY event mentioned, record the index range (start–end) where it
  occurs — never describe a feature with only a duration ("across X indices")
  and no anchor. Make comparisons between expert and player for each event found.
    - Is the initiation "abrupt" or "gradual"?
    - Is the release "trail braking" or an "abrupt drop"?
    - Is there any "modulation dip" (dip-and-recover) during initiation, hold, or release?
    - Are there flat holds at peak?

3. Enumerate ONLY clearly-visible events (brake initiation, brake
   release, peak brake pressure, modulation dip).  Do not invent
   events to satisfy a template — if a trace has one initiation and
   one release, report one and one.  Skip any event you cannot clearly
   distinguish from the grid.  For every event record the index on
   each trace.

4. Classify the segment using exactly one segment-class glossary term
   (flat-full segment, flat-zero segment, brake-only segment,
   release-only segment, brake-and-release segment, multi-cycle
   segment, unmatch segment).

5. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide.  Lead with the shape difference
  (initiation and release character, modulation dips, flat holds) anchored to
  approximate index ranges; then the matched events with the index on each trace; then the
  segment class callout; then a trail-braking callout; then any throttle/brake overlap.
  All indices and y-values are approximate.

glossary.flat-full segment: Both traces remain at peak brake (~1.0) across the segment; no release or re-initiation is visible.
glossary.flat-zero segment: Both traces remain near zero across the segment; the segment is a coast or throttle zone rather than a braking phase.
glossary.brake-only segment: A brake initiation is visible but the segment ends before any release.
glossary.release-only segment: A brake release is visible but no prior initiation falls inside the window (segment starts mid-corner with brake already applied).
glossary.brake-and-release segment: A full initiation → peak hold → release cycle is visible within the segment (the typical case).
glossary.multi-cycle segment: More than one initiation / release pair is visible (e.g. chicane or a series of esses) in travel order.
glossary.unmatch segment: One trace stays flat while the other is active, or the two traces do not share the same overall shape.
glossary.brake initiation: Brake pressure rises from near zero and ends at a peak value without dropping back to zero immediately, with the positive slope potentially containing some fluctuation. The modulation dip may occur during the initiation, but the overall slope should be positive. When compare a subject to an object, a subject has more abrupt initiations if the length of the rise is shorter; more gradual initiations if the rise is longer.
glossary.brake release: Brake pressure drops from a consistent value and ends at near zero, with the negative slope potentially containing some fluctuation. The modulation dip may occur during the release, but the overall slope should be negative. When compare a subject to an object, a subject has more abrupt releases if the length of the drop is shorter; more gradual releases (i.e. trail braking) if the drop is longer.
glossary.initiation onset: start of brake initiation. An index where the brake trace first rises above ~0.05 from zero with a positive slope. A subject has an earlier initiation onset if this index is less than the other trace's initiation onset; later if it is greater.
glossary.release onset: start of brake release. An index where the brake trace first begins dropping from a consistent value with a negative slope. A subject has an earlier release onset if this index is less than the other trace's release onset; later if it is greater.
glossary.peak brake pressure: The maximum y-value the brake trace reaches across the segment, with the index (or index range, if held).
glossary.modulation dip: Occurs in mid-initiation up slope, mid-hold, or mid-release down slope where the trace dips and recovers without returning to zero. When modulation dip occurs for both traces at about the same index, compare the depth and duration of the dips to determine whether the subject shows a more pronounced dip.
glossary.trail braking: A release where brake pressure decreases gradually over many indices rather than dropping abruptly to zero.  Used to maintain front-axle load through corner entry.
glossary.abrupt drop: A subject releases brake where the brake trace returns to zero faster than the object's. The slope is more negative and the release duration is shorter. Used to describe a non-trail-braking release.
glossary.applies throttle and brake simultaneousl: An index range where both the brake trace and the throttle trace are above zero at the same time.  Report approximate start, end, and approximate values, and whether the expert shows the same overlap.

brake.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph.  Every event MUST be anchored to an index range or specific index — never quote a duration alone.
(a) Detailed events — emit ONLY the clauses your chosen class
    requires.  Use whichever apply:

  - event comparison (initiation, release, initiation onset, release onset,
    peak brake pressure):
    "<event>: <Player/expert> starts at index A and ends at index B (if there is one);
    <Player/expert> starts at index C and ends at index D (if there is one) — <Player/expert>
    is <earlier|later> by N indices, or <more abrupt|more gradual> by M
    indices, or <higher|lower> by Δ pressure."

    Modulation dip occurs within the initiation, hold, or release. When present
    on either trace, include as a separate clause:
      "<Player/expert>'s <initiation|hold|release> across indices X–Y contains
      a modulation dip around index N (val); <other>'s <initiation|hold|release>
      across the same range is monotonic."
    If both traces dip near the same index, compare depth and duration:
      "Both <initiation|hold|release> slopes contain a modulation dip near
      index N; <Player/expert>'s dip is <deeper|shallower> by Δ and
      <longer|shorter> by D indices."

  - Trail-braking callout (when applicable):
    "Expert trail-brakes across indices X–Y; player <trail-brakes
     across indices P–Q | shows an abrupt drop at index R>."

(b) Segment class callout (when applicable):
  "The segment is a <segment-class> — both traces <description of
   shape features that define the class, anchored to index ranges>."
common_description_errors: Verify expert vs player from the legend before describing
Refer to each trace as "player" or "expert" in the output — NEVER by its colour (no "the blue trace", "the red line", etc.).  Colour is only an internal cue for reading the legend
Do NOT invent events to fill the format. If a trace has only one initiation and one release, report only one of each
Anchor EVERY shape feature and event to an index range or specific index — "gradual across 5 indices" without a start index is insufficient; write "gradual across indices 45–50" instead
Suspect-pattern check: if your output shows the player consistently offset by exactly the same number of indices at every event, you are pattern-matching rather than reading — restart from step 3
For a flat-zero segment, state the classification rather than fabricating an initiation or release
Do NOT confuse brake release (trace returning to zero) with brake reduction (decreasing but not to zero — i.e. trail braking)

time_delta.title: Time Difference to Expert

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)
axes.y: Time delta in milliseconds (positive = player behind expert, negative = player ahead)

visual_elements: Single line trace showing cumulative time gap
Rising line means the gap is growing (player falling further behind)
Falling line means the gap is shrinking (player catching up)
Flat line means the gap is stable
time_delta.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Read the y-axis scale and range; note the sign convention
   (+ = player behind expert, − = player ahead).

2. Perform piecewise linear segmentation of the trace, scanning
   left → right starting at the leftmost index.  Begin a new
   segment at every breakpoint — i.e. wherever the local slope
   (Δms / Δindex) deviates from the slope of the current
   segment.  Within a segment the slope is approximately
   constant (linear regime).  Ignore micro-jitter that does not
   change the segment's slope.

3. For each slope segment record:
     - index range (start–end)
     - ms value at start and end
     - slope ratio (Δms / Δindex, signed)
     - slope tag from glossary: "gap grows" (slope > 0),
       "gap shrinks" (slope < 0), "gap holds stable" (slope ≈ 0)
     - who is ahead/behind at each endpoint (sign of ms)

4. Compute the overall gap across the whole segment: compare ms
   at the right edge vs the left edge.  Tag as "overall gap
   grows" (right > left), "overall gap shrinks" (right < left),
   or "overall gap holds stable" (|right − left| within scale
   noise).  Record both endpoint ms values.

Compose: 5. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide — one sentence
   per slope segment from step 3 in travel order (index range,
   ms endpoints, slope tag, who is ahead/behind), then a closing
   overall-gap sentence from step 4.

glossary.slope segment: A contiguous index range over which the slope ratio (Δms per index) of the time-delta trace is approximately constant. Segment boundaries are kinks where the slope ratio visibly changes.  Each segment carries exactly one slope tag (gap grows / gap shrinks / gap holds stable).
glossary.slope ratio: Signed rate of change of the time delta: (ms_end − ms_start) / (index_end − index_start) for the segment.  Positive = gap growing; negative = gap shrinking; near zero = stable.
glossary.gap grows: Slope tag for a segment with positive slope ratio — player falls further behind expert across the segment (ms increases).
glossary.gap shrinks: Slope tag for a segment with negative slope ratio — player catches up to expert across the segment (ms decreases).
glossary.gap holds stable: Slope tag for a segment with slope ratio ≈ 0 — neither trace gains time on the other across the segment (ms roughly flat).
glossary.overall gap grows: Whole-segment tag: ms at the right edge is greater than ms at the left edge — the player ended the segment further behind the expert than they started (net time lost).
glossary.overall gap shrinks: Whole-segment tag: ms at the right edge is less than ms at the left edge — the player ended the segment closer to (or further ahead of) the expert than they started (net time gained).
glossary.overall gap holds stable: Whole-segment tag: ms at the right edge is approximately equal to ms at the left edge — no net time change across the segment, even if intermediate slope segments swung.

time_delta.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph.  Subject = player, object = expert.
(a) One sentence per slope segment in travel order:
  "Across indices <N>–<M> the gap <grows|shrinks|holds stable>
   from <X> ms to <Y> ms (slope <Δms/Δidx>) — player is
   <behind|ahead> by <|X|> ms at index <N> and <behind|ahead> by
   <|Y|> ms at index <M>."

(b) Closing overall-gap sentence (always emit, after the
    per-segment sentences):
  "Overall the gap <grows|shrinks|holds stable> across the
   segment, from <X_left> ms at index <0> to <X_right> ms at
   index <end> (net <±Δ> ms)."
common_description_errors: Do NOT skip the per-segment breakdown and only report the overall gap — both are required (per-segment sentences first, overall sentence last)
Do NOT skip the closing overall-gap sentence — every output must end with it
Overall tag is computed from left-edge vs right-edge ms only — do NOT derive it from how many segments grow vs shrink
Do NOT split on micro-jitter — only on visible kinks where the slope ratio clearly changes
Do NOT ignore the SCALE of the y-axis — a 0.01 s change is negligible, a 0.5 s change is large
Do NOT confuse the sign — positive means player is BEHIND expert, negative means AHEAD
Do NOT interpret why the delta changes — describe WHAT the line does

speed_delta.title: Speed Difference (Expert - Player)

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)
axes.y: Speed difference in km/h (positive = expert faster, negative = player faster)

visual_elements: Single line trace oscillating around zero
Values above zero mean the expert is faster than the player at that index
Values below zero mean the player is faster than the expert at that index
speed_delta.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Read the y-axis range and units (km/h); note the sign convention
   (+ = expert faster, − = player faster).
2. Record the overall bias — who is faster across most of the segment. 3. Walk the trace; for each notable section record the index range
   (start–end), the sign, and the peak |delta| with its index.
4. Record every index where the line crosses zero.
Match (apply glossary thresholds): 5. For each section in step 3, decide whether it is a "speed match"
   (|delta| ≤ 5 km/h), a "large gap section" (|delta| > 20 km/h), or
   a regular gap.  Tag each zero-crossing index from step 4.

Compose: 6. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide — one sentence per
   section, embedding the matched glossary tag (where applicable),
   the index range, the km/h values, and who is faster.

glossary.speed match: A section where |delta| stays within ±5 km/h — player matches the expert's speed.  Report start index, end index, and peak |delta|.
glossary.large gap section: A section where |delta| exceeds 20 km/h.  Report start index, end index, peak magnitude, and direction (expert faster or player faster).
glossary.zero crossing: An index where the speed-delta line crosses zero.  Report whether the flip is from expert-faster to player-faster or vice versa.

speed_delta.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph, one sentence per section.  Plain-English direction (expert faster / player faster) carries the sign; glossary tags carry the threshold.
  "<who-is-faster> by <N km/h> across indices <A>–<B>
   (peak <Z km/h> at index <I>) [— large gap section / speed match]."
Example: "Expert faster than player by up to 28 km/h across indices 65–85 (peak 28 km/h at index 75) — large gap section; zero crossing at index 92 flips to player faster; player faster than expert by 6 km/h across indices 110–130."
common_description_errors: Do NOT read the sign backwards — positive = expert faster, negative = player faster
Do NOT describe brief single-index spikes as sustained gaps
Do NOT interpret causation — describe the speed difference and tag thresholds with glossary terms

speed.title: Speed Trace: Expert vs Player

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)

visual_elements: Two overlapping line traces — expert_optimal_speed and Physics_speed_kmh (player)
Legend identifies which line is which
Speed dropping = deceleration / braking; speed minimum = apex / lowest speed; speed rising = acceleration
speed.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Name who is who: read the legend; assign each colour to expert or player. 2. For each trace independently, locate four glossary anchor points:
   entry speed (left edge), deceleration onset, minimum speed point,
   acceleration onset, exit speed (right edge).  Record the index
   and km/h at each.
3. Note shape mismatches on either trace — flat spot, plateau, or
   extended low-speed range — with the index range.

Match (line up player vs expert, anchor by anchor): 4. For each glossary anchor, pair player-value vs expert-value (km/h
   offset) and player-index vs expert-index (timing offset); record
   the sign of each gap.

Compose: 5. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide — one sentence per
   anchor, embedding both km/h values and the directional offset.
   Add a sentence for any shape mismatch from step 3.

glossary.deceleration onset: The index where the speed trace begins falling from its entry value — start of the braking phase.
glossary.minimum speed point: The index where the speed trace bottoms out, plus the km/h value at that point.
glossary.acceleration onset: The index where the speed trace begins rising again from the minimum — start of the corner-exit acceleration phase.

speed.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph, one sentence per glossary anchor + entry/exit edges. Subject = player, object = expert.
  "The player's <anchor> is at index <A> (<X km/h>); the expert's at
   index <C> (<Y km/h>) — player is <earlier|later|higher|lower> by
   <N> indices / <Δ> km/h."
Example: "The player's minimum speed point is at index 92 (78 km/h); the expert's at index 86 (95 km/h) — player is later by 6 indices and lower by 17 km/h."
common_description_errors: Do NOT compare values from different anchors — entry vs entry, minimum vs minimum, exit vs exit
Do NOT confuse the two traces — verify which line is which from the legend
Refer to each trace as "player" or "expert" in the output — NEVER by its colour (no "the blue trace", "the red line", etc.).  Colour is only an internal cue for reading the legend
Do NOT report a player speed without also reporting the expert's speed at the matching anchor and the directional offset
Do NOT skip reporting numerical values — always state km/h figures

push_limit.title: Driver Push/Limit

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)

visual_elements: Single line trace showing tire grip utilisation over the segment
Reference threshold at y = 1.0 — tire is operating exactly at its peak
Values below 1.0 = grip headroom remaining; values above 1.0 = over-limit
Only excursions above 1.0 count as spikes
push_limit.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Note: player's trace only — no expert comparison; y = 1.0 is the
   threshold.
2. Record the baseline level (well below / near / riding at 1.0) and
   its index range.
3. For every excursion above 1.0, record the index range
   (onset–peak–return) and the peak value.
4. If the trace never crosses 1.0, record the segment-wide max value
   and its index.

Match (apply glossary tags to each section): 5. For each above-1.0 excursion from step 3, decide whether it is an
   "over-limit spike" (brief, one peak) or "sustained over-limit"
   (stays above 1.0 for many indices — use the long-form
   "tire is sustained over its peak grip").
6. For each baseline section from step 2, decide whether it qualifies
   as "sustained low" (well below 1.0).

Compose: 7. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide — one clause per
   tagged section, embedding the glossary term plus the index range
   and peak/baseline values.

glossary.over-limit spike: A brief excursion above 1.0 (one peak, returns within a few indices).  Report start index (crossing 1.0), peak index, peak value, return index, and duration above 1.0.
glossary.sustained over-limit: A section where the trace stays above 1.0 for many indices (not a single-index spike).  Use the long-form phrasing "tire is sustained over its peak grip across indices N–M".
glossary.tire is sustained over its peak grip: Long-form phrasing for sustained over-limit.  Report the index range and the peak value.
glossary.sustained low: A section where the trace stays well below 1.0 — driver is well within grip limits.  Report the index range and the approximate value held.

push_limit.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph.  Player's trace only; reference = y = 1.0 (no expert comparison).
  "<glossary tag> across indices <N>–<M> (peak <value> at index <X>;
   returns to 1.0 at index <Y>, duration <D> indices)."
Example: "The player's trace shows an over-limit spike across indices 72–88 (peaks at 1.18 at index 80, returns at 88, 16 indices); sustained low across indices 0–71 (~0.85)." If never above 1.0: "The player's trace stays at or below 1.0 across the full segment (max <value> at index <X>); no spike to report."
common_description_errors: Do NOT call a rise a 'spike' unless it exceeds 1.0 — values below 1.0 mean grip is still within peak
Do NOT measure spikes against the local baseline — 1.0 is the reference
Do NOT confuse this metric with speed — push_limit is about tire grip utilisation
Do NOT interpret over-limit spikes as oversteer or understeer — that requires the slip balance graph

gear.title: Gear Selection: Expert vs Player

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)

visual_elements: Two step-like traces — expert_optimal_gear and Physics_gear (player)
Legend identifies which line is which
Traces jump between integer levels (no smooth curves)
Grid lines for reading exact gear numbers and shift indices
gear.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Name who is who: read the legend; assign each colour to expert or player. 2. Walk left-to-right; for each trace record every shift event
   (direction = upshift|downshift, source gear → target gear, index)
   and the gear-hold range that follows (indices start–end, gear).
3. Tally total shifts on each trace.
Match (line up player vs expert): 4. Pair each shift event with its matching event on the other trace
   (same direction, similar index); record the index gap and sign.
5. Identify index ranges where the two traces hold different gear
   numbers; record range and the two gear values.

Compose: 6. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide — one sentence per
   paired shift, one sentence per gear-hold mismatch range, plus a
   closing tally line if shift counts differ.
gear.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph. Subject = player, object = expert. For paired shift events:
  "Player <upshift|downshift> <N>→<M> at index <A>; expert at
   index <B> — player is <earlier|later> by <|A−B|> indices."
For gear-hold mismatch ranges:
  "Across indices <X>–<Y> the player holds gear <N> while the expert
   holds gear <M> — player is in a <lower|higher> gear."
Example: "Player upshift 3→4 at index 110; expert at index 102 — player is later by 8 indices.  Across indices 102–110 the player holds gear 3 while the expert holds gear 4 — player is in a lower gear."
common_description_errors: Gear values are INTEGERS — do not report fractional gear numbers
Refer to each trace as "player" or "expert" in the output — NEVER by its colour (no "the blue trace", "the red line", etc.).  Colour is only an internal cue for reading the legend
Do NOT confuse upshift and downshift — up = higher gear number, down = lower gear number
Do NOT report a player shift without also reporting the expert's matching shift and the index gap
Do NOT interpret gear choices — describe what you see

trajectory_balance.title: Oversteer/Understeer Slip Balance

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)
axes.y: Rear − Front mean |slip angle| (rad).  0 = balanced; positive = oversteer; negative = understeer.  ONLY |y| > 0.02 rad counts as oversteer/understeer; |y| ≤ 0.02 is neutral.

visual_elements: Single black line trace = signed slip balance (mean |rear slip| − mean |front slip|) over segment index
RED shading between the trace and y = 0 wherever the trace is above zero (oversteer / rear-slip dominant)
BLUE shading between the trace and y = 0 wherever the trace is below zero (understeer / front-slip dominant)
Grey dashed horizontal reference line at y = 0 (balanced)
Grid lines for reading approximate values
No expert trace — player only.  Magnitude is shown directly on the y-axis
trajectory_balance.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Neutral band = |y| ≤ 0.02 rad.  y > +0.02 = oversteer;
   y < −0.02 = understeer; otherwise neutral.
2. Walk the trace left-to-right and split it into contiguous
   sections by band.  For each section record:
     - tag (oversteer / understeer / neutral)
     - start index (first index in the section)
     - end index (last index in the section)
     - peak/trough value (rad) for oversteer/understeer sections

Classify (relate sections, do not just enumerate): 3. Identify the primary slide: the oversteer or understeer
   section with the visually tallest peak.  This is the major
   handling event for the segment.
4. For every other oversteer/understeer section, decide whether
   it is a recovery transient or a standalone event using two
   visual checks (no arithmetic — compare what you see):
     - Height: is its peak clearly much shorter than the
       primary slide's peak, or comparable in height?
     - Gap: does the trace pass through a sustained neutral run
       between the primary slide and this section, or does it
       flip sign and immediately return?
   Much shorter peak AND no sustained neutral between → recovery
   transient (driver's correction settling, not a separate
   balance event).  Comparable peak AND sustained neutral
   between → standalone event.  Comparable-height opposite-sign
   bump immediately after the primary with no sustained neutral
   → true secondary slide (tank-slapper) — flag it, do not hide.
5. When the primary slide is oversteer and the next transient is
   understeer with no sustained neutral between, tag the
   transient as an over-correction (driver counter-steered past
   neutral; fronts momentarily slip more than rears).  A small
   same-sign oversteer bump that follows is the pendulum swinging
   back — also a recovery transient of the same event.

Compose: 6. Write the summary using sentence_format_guide — one clause
   for the primary slide with its recovery transients attached,
   one clause per standalone event, and neutral runs between
   events.

glossary.oversteer: Contiguous range where y > +0.02 rad (rear-slip dominant). Report start index, end index, and peak value (rad).
glossary.understeer: Contiguous range where y < −0.02 rad (front-slip dominant). Report start index, end index, and trough value (most negative rad).
glossary.neutral: Contiguous range where |y| ≤ 0.02 rad — front and rear slip balanced.  Report start index and end index.
glossary.primary slide: The oversteer or understeer section with the visually tallest peak in the segment — the major handling event to report. Anchored by index range and peak value.
glossary.recovery transient: A smaller oversteer or understeer section close to the primary slide, with a peak clearly much shorter than the primary's AND no sustained neutral run between it and the primary.  Reflects the driver catching the slide rather than a separate balance event; reported as part of the primary slide's clause, not standalone.
glossary.over-correction: Specific recovery-transient case where the primary slide is oversteer and the trace immediately dips into understeer before returning.  The dip is the driver counter-steering past neutral (fronts slipping more than rears for an instant), not cornering understeer.  A small same-sign oversteer bump may follow as the pendulum swings back; both are transients of the same event.
glossary.standalone event: An oversteer or understeer section that is NOT a recovery transient — its peak is comparable in height to the primary slide, or a sustained neutral run separates it from the primary.  Reported on its own clause.
glossary.true secondary slide: A comparable-height opposite-sign bump immediately after the primary with no sustained neutral between — a real tank-slapper rather than a recovery transient.  Reported as its own primary- level clause and flagged as a secondary slide.

trajectory_balance.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph. Player's trace only; reference = ±0.02 rad neutral band (no expert comparison). Every clause MUST be anchored to an index range. Skeleton: Primary slide (always emit first when present): "Primary across indices – (peak rad)." Recovery transient(s) attached to the primary (when present): "...followed by an over-correction transient across indices

– (peak rad)" (for OS primary → US dip), and/or "...and a same-sign recovery bump across indices – (peak rad)" (pendulum swing back). Standalone event (separated from the primary by a sustained neutral run, or comparable in peak height): " across indices – (peak rad)." True secondary slide (comparable-height opposite-sign bump immediately after the primary, no sustained neutral between): "Secondary slide: across indices – (peak rad) — comparable height to the primary with no neutral run between." Neutral: "Neutral across indices –."
common_description_errors: Do NOT count |y| ≤ 0.02 rad as oversteer or understeer — that is the neutral band; only ranges beyond ±0.02 qualify
Do NOT confuse the sign — y > +0.02 (red) = oversteer; y < −0.02 (blue) = understeer

Refer to regions as "oversteer", "understeer", or "neutral" in the output — NEVER by their shading colour (no "the red shading", "the blue band", etc.).  Colour is only an internal cue for reading the sign
Do NOT invent an expert trace — this graph shows the player only
Anchor EVERY section to an index range (start–end)
Do NOT interpret the colours as good or bad — describe what is shown (sign, magnitude, index range)
Do NOT skip describing neutral sections
Do NOT report a small opposite-sign section immediately after a large oversteer as standalone understeer — describe it as an over-correction transient of the primary slide

Do NOT report every contiguous section as an equal event — focus on the primary slide (the major handling problem); recovery transients are driver corrections settling and collapse into the primary's clause
Do NOT use numeric ratios or arithmetic to decide transient vs standalone — compare peak heights visually and check whether a sustained neutral run separates the sections

trajectory_detailed.title: Detailed Trajectory

axes.y: Track Y position (spatial, not time-based — y-axis is INVERTED)

visual_elements: Green solid line = Player trajectory
Blue dashed line = Expert (optimal) trajectory
Black X marker = Start of segment
Black O marker = End of segment
Yellow circle on expert line = Entry (turn-in onset)
Red star on expert line = Apex (geometric — peak |κ| of expert line)
Green triangle on expert line = Exit (track-out, where curvature subsides)
Numbered apex labels (#1, #2, …) appear on chicanes / esses — one set of entry/apex/exit markers per arc
Y-axis is INVERTED (standard for top-down track views)
trajectory_detailed.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record positions, do not summarise yet): 1. Verify who is who: player = green solid, expert = blue dashed;
   confirm direction of travel from Start (X) → End (O).
2. Read the 'Phase Indices (expert-anchored)' attachment.  Each
   element of phase_indices.phases is one detected arc with expert
   entry / apex / exit indices.  Empty list ⇒ no corner; describe the
   overall shape only and stop.  Chicanes / esses iterate phases
   0, 1, … of opposite 'direction' — repeat steps 3–5 per phase k.

For each phase k, walk the corner in order.
3. ENTRY — at the expert's entry index:
   (a) "Initiate the turn" (event): does the player's line begin
       curving earlier (upstream) or later (downstream) than the
       expert's entry index, or at the same index?
   (b) "Entry trajectory" (shape): walk both curves from expert's
       entry index to expert's apex index.  Through that span, does
       the player's line bulge outside the expert's, cut inside
       the expert's, or track the expert's?
4. APEX — at the expert's apex index (red star):
   (a) "Reach apex" (event): does the player's tightest point
       land earlier, later, or at the expert's apex index?
   (b) "Apex" (shape): at expert's apex index, is the player's
       curve closer to inside / closer to outside / aligned?
5. EXIT — at the expert's exit index (green triangle):
   (a) "Time to exit" (event): does the player begin unwinding
       the line earlier, later, or at the expert's exit index?
   (b) "Exit trajectory" (shape): walk both curves from expert's
       apex index through and past expert's exit index.  Through
       that span, does the player's line bulge outside, cut
       inside, or track the expert?  Flag if it runs wide of
       normal track-out.

6. Divergence enumeration: list EVERY span (entry-to-apex and
   apex-to-exit, for each phase k) where the player's line shows
   a visible gap from the expert's.  Do NOT pick just the biggest
   one — every span with a non-'tracks' verdict goes in the list.
   If a single span shows the player both bulging outside and
   cutting inside at different points along it, record both
   observations for that span.  Also list any visible event-term
   offsets (player initiates the turn / reaches apex / times the
   exit earlier or later than expert).
7. Compose using sentence_format_guide — one paragraph per phase,
   six clauses (one event + one shape per beat), plus a final
   enumeration line that lists every divergence found in step 6.
   Reference markers using "at expert's <entry|apex|exit>
   (idx N, phase k)" for events and "between expert's
   <entry|apex> and <apex|exit> (idx N → idx M, phase k)" for
   shape spans.

glossary.Initiate the turn: Event term.  The index on the player's line where the curve first deviates from a straight path into the corner, compared to the expert's entry index (yellow circle on expert line).  Report as earlier (upstream of expert entry) / later (downstream) / at the same index.
glossary.Entry trajectory: Shape term.  The player's curve across the SPAN from expert's entry index to expert's apex index — the entire turn-in half of the corner, not a single point.  Report as bulges outside / cuts inside / tracks the expert.  Single-index verdicts at the entry marker alone are NOT acceptable; the lines almost always meet at the marker, so visible divergence lives mid-span.
glossary.Reach apex: Event term.  The index on the player's line where the curve is at its tightest point, compared to the expert's apex index (red star on expert line — argmax(|κ|) of the smoothed expert position trace).  Report as earlier / later / at the same index.
glossary.Apex: Shape term.  The position of the player's curve at expert's apex index.  Report as closer to inside (tighter line) / closer to outside (wider line) / aligned with the expert.
glossary.Time to exit: Event term.  The index on the player's line where the curve begins to unwind and open back out, compared to the expert's exit index (green triangle on expert line — where |κ| drops back below the noise threshold).  Report as earlier / later / at the same index.
glossary.Exit trajectory: Shape term.  The player's curve across the SPAN from expert's apex index through and past expert's exit index — the entire track-out half of the corner.  Report as bulges outside / cuts inside / tracks the expert; flag if the player's line runs wide of normal track-out.  Single-index verdicts at the exit marker alone are NOT acceptable.
glossary.at expert's entry (idx N, phase k): The 'entry' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — arc onset where |κ| of the smoothed expert position trace first clears the noise threshold (turn-in).  k is 0 for a single-arc corner; chicanes / esses iterate all phases.  Substitute the actual idx N and arc index k.
glossary.at expert's apex (idx N, phase k): The 'apex' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — argmax(|κ|) within the arc on the smoothed expert position trace (geometric apex; can differ from minimum-speed index on flat-speed sweepers and trail-braked corners).  Substitute the actual idx N and arc index k.
glossary.at expert's exit (idx N, phase k): The 'exit' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — arc offset where |κ| drops back below the noise threshold (track-out).  Substitute the actual idx N and arc index k.

trajectory_detailed.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph per phase k from phase_indices.phases, plus a final 'overall' line.  Subject = player, object = expert.
Each paragraph has three beats (entry, apex, exit), and each beat contributes two sentences: one event sentence, one shape sentence. Event sentences anchor to a single index; shape sentences for entry and exit anchor to a SPAN between markers.

  Event:  "Player initiates the turn <earlier than | later than |
           at> expert's entry (idx N, phase k)."
  Shape:  "Player's entry trajectory between expert's entry
           (idx N) and apex (idx M, phase k) <bulges outside |
           cuts inside | tracks> the expert."
  Event:  "Player reaches apex <earlier than | later than | at>
           expert's apex (idx M, phase k)."
  Shape:  "Player's apex at expert's apex (idx M, phase k) is
           <closer to inside | closer to outside | aligned with>
           the expert."
  Event:  "Player times the exit <earlier than | later than | at>
           expert's exit (idx P, phase k)."
  Shape:  "Player's exit trajectory between expert's apex (idx M)
           and exit (idx P, phase k) <bulges outside | cuts
           inside | tracks> the expert."

Final enumeration line — list EVERY span with a non-'tracks' verdict and EVERY event-offset (earlier/later, not 'at') from the per-phase paragraphs.  Skip only when no divergence at all was observed across any phase.  Suggested skeleton (one clause per finding, comma-separated):
  "Divergences: phase k entry-to-apex bulges outside, phase k
   apex-to-exit cuts inside, player reaches apex earlier than
   expert at idx M phase k, …"
common_description_errors: The Y-AXIS IS INVERTED — do not read up/down wrong when describing inside/outside of corners
Do NOT assume a direction of travel — verify from the Start (X) and End (O) markers

Refer to the lines as "player" and "expert", and to markers as "entry", "apex", and "exit" in the output — NEVER by their colour or shape (no "the green line", "the blue dashed line", "the red star", "the yellow circle", etc.).  Colour and marker shape are only internal cues for identifying which trace and marker is which
Do NOT describe the player line in isolation — for every phase state position RELATIVE to the expert line

Do NOT conflate event terms with shape terms — 'Initiate the turn / Reach apex / Time to exit' describe WHEN (index compared to expert), 'Entry / Apex / Exit trajectory' describe WHERE (line position relative to expert)

Do NOT default to 'aligned' or 'tracks the expert' when the lines visibly diverge anywhere in the span. Markers are typically the most-aligned points; search the SPAN BETWEEN markers for bulges. Banned hedging phrases: 'closely following', 'no significant deviation', 'minimal', 'smooth and precise', 'does not significantly impact'.

Do NOT collapse the divergence callout to the single biggest gap — enumerate EVERY span with a visible difference and EVERY event-offset (earlier/later). 'The largest divergence is …' is wrong by construction; the goal is to find all differences, not rank them.
Do NOT describe tiny pixel-level differences as meaningful — only report clear, visible separations
Phase indices come from the attachment, not from the graph itself — if no 'Phase Indices (expert-anchored)' attachment is present, describe shapes only

trajectory_gas_brake.title: Gas/Brake Trajectory
visual_elements: Player trajectory as a COLOURED line (colour = throttle/brake balance)
GREEN sections = full throttle (gas dominant)
RED sections = full brake
YELLOW sections = coasting (neither gas nor brake, or partial overlap)
Colour bar on the side maps values: -1 (full brake) through 0 (coast) to +1 (full gas)
Blue/grey dashed line = Expert trajectory (reference position)
Black X = Start, Black O = End
trajectory_gas_brake.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record positions and colours, do not summarise yet): 1. Verify who is who: player = coloured line, expert = blue/grey
   dashed; confirm direction of travel from Start (X) → End (O).
2. If the 'Phase Indices (expert-anchored)' attachment is present
   on this prompt, read its phase_indices.phases list — one element
   per arc, each with entry / apex / exit indices.  Empty list ⇒
   describe colours and positions without anchoring to phases.
3. Walk the player's coloured line in travel order; record each
   colour section as (colour, start idx, end idx, phase k +
   entry|apex|exit if it falls in or near a phase index).
4. For each phase k, at each of its entry / apex / exit indices,
   record the player's colour AT that idx and where the player's
   curve sits relative to the expert's curve — closer to inside /
   closer to outside / aligned.

Match (apply glossary tags to ambiguous colours): 5. For every yellow section from step 3, tag it
   "yellow (coast or overlap)" — the colour cannot disambiguate coast
   from partial gas/brake overlap.

Compose: 6. Write one paragraph using sentence_format_guide.  Two halves:
   (a) colour sequence in travel order, anchored to each phase k
       when phases are present, with every yellow tagged
       "yellow (coast or overlap)";
   (b) trajectory position at each phase's entry / apex / exit —
       closer to inside / closer to outside / aligned, using
       "at expert's <entry|apex|exit> (idx N, phase k)" form.

glossary.yellow (coast or overlap): A yellow section on the gas/brake trajectory.  The colour cannot disambiguate coast from partial gas/brake overlap; use this exact phrasing rather than guessing.
glossary.at expert's entry (idx N, phase k): The 'entry' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — arc onset where |κ| of the smoothed expert position trace first clears the noise threshold (turn-in).  k is 0 for a single-arc corner; chicanes / esses iterate all phases.  Substitute the actual idx N and arc index k.
glossary.at expert's apex (idx N, phase k): The 'apex' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — argmax(|κ|) within the arc on the smoothed expert position trace (geometric apex; can differ from minimum-speed index on flat-speed sweepers and trail-braked corners).  Substitute the actual idx N and arc index k.
glossary.at expert's exit (idx N, phase k): The 'exit' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — arc offset where |κ| drops back below the noise threshold (track-out).  Substitute the actual idx N and arc index k.

trajectory_gas_brake.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph, two halves.
(a) Colour sequence skeleton (anchor each section to its phase k
    when phases are present):
  "Player: <colour> at phase <k> <entry|apex|exit> (idx <A>–<B>)
   → <colour> at phase <k> <entry|apex|exit> (idx <C>–<D>) → ..."
Tag every yellow with "yellow (coast or overlap)".
(b) Position-at-phase skeleton (one clause per (phase k, marker)
    combination):
  "At expert's <entry|apex|exit> (idx N, phase k) the player's
   curve is <colour> and <closer to inside | closer to outside |
   aligned with> the expert."

Example (single-phase corner, k = 0): "Player: green on straight (idx 0–55) → red at phase 0 entry (idx 55–82) → yellow (coast or overlap) at phase 0 apex area (idx 82–95) → green at phase 0 exit (idx 95–end).  At expert's entry (idx 50, phase 0) the player's curve is green and aligned with the expert.  At expert's apex (idx 88, phase 0) the player's curve is yellow (coast or overlap) and closer to outside than the expert."
common_description_errors: Do NOT describe colour transitions without relating them to the corner geometry
Yellow does NOT necessarily mean 'nothing is happening' — phrase it as 'yellow (coast or overlap)'
Do NOT describe what the colour pattern means for performance — describe colours, locations, and marker references only
Do NOT forget to describe the trajectory POSITION (closer to inside / closer to outside) in addition to the colours

trajectory_offset.title: Trajectory Offset (signed)

axes.x: Segment index (time progression left to right)
axes.y: Lateral offset in metres — positive = player wider than expert (toward outside of corner); negative = tighter (toward inside)

visual_elements: Single green line trace = signed lateral offset (player vs expert)
Gray dashed horizontal line at y = 0 = expert reference (zero offset)
Yellow circle on the offset trace = Entry (turn-in, expert-anchored)
Red star on the offset trace = Apex (peak |κ| of expert line)
Green triangle on the offset trace = Exit (track-out)
Numbered apex labels (#1, #2, …) on chicanes / esses — one set of entry/apex/exit per arc
trajectory_offset.how_to_analyze:
Observe (record values, do not summarise yet): 1. Read 'phase_indices.phases' from the 'Phase Indices (expert-
   anchored)' attachment:
     - Non-empty list: each element is one detected arc.  Run
       step 2 once per arc, in travel order (chicanes /
       esses produce multiple arcs).
     - Empty list: the segment has no corner.  Report whether
       the offset trace stays near zero or drifts away (peak
       trajectory offset and its index) and stop.

2. For each phase k, walk the four corner-span beats in
   travel order — approach-to-entry, entry-to-apex,
   apex-to-exit, post-exit (see glossary for each beat's
   span definition).  For each beat: read the SLOPE of the
   offset trace across the beat's span and pick one slope
   tag — moving toward negative (tightening), moving toward
   positive (widening), aligned, or recovery (see glossary).
   Record the peak trajectory offset and its index inside
   the beat's span.

3. Divergence enumeration: list EVERY beat across all phases
   whose tag is NOT "aligned".

4. Compose using sentence_format_guide.

glossary.approach-to-entry: First of four corner-span beats.  Span runs from segment start → this phase's expert entry, OR (on chicanes / esses) from the previous phase's expert exit → this phase's expert entry.
glossary.entry-to-apex: Second of four corner-span beats.  Span runs from this phase's expert entry → this phase's expert apex.
glossary.apex-to-exit: Third of four corner-span beats.  Span runs from this phase's expert apex → this phase's expert exit.
glossary.post-exit: Fourth and final corner-span beat.  Span runs from this phase's expert exit → the next phase's expert entry, OR (on the last phase) → segment end.  "recovery" tag applies when the offset trace trends back toward zero across this span.
glossary.moving toward negative (tightening): Tag for a span where the offset trace becomes more negative (slope downward, trajectory offset growing below zero) — player's line is tightening relative to the expert.
glossary.moving toward positive (widening): Tag for a span where the offset trace becomes more positive (slope upward, trajectory offset growing above zero) — player's line is widening relative to the expert.
glossary.aligned: Tag for a span where the offset trace stays within roughly ±0.5 m of zero — no meaningful lateral deviation from the expert.
glossary.recovery: Tag for a span where trajectory offset trends back toward zero from either side (a positive value decreasing toward zero, or a negative value increasing toward zero) — the player is closing the lateral gap.  Most common on the post-exit span; corresponds to the "merging back to expert line" phrase (label RM7).
glossary.wider than expert: Offset value above zero at a specific index — player's line is to the OUTSIDE of the corner relative to the expert.
glossary.tighter than expert: Offset value below zero at a specific index — player's line is to the INSIDE of the corner relative to the expert.
glossary.at expert's entry (index N, phase k): The 'entry' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — arc onset where |κ| of the smoothed expert position trace first clears the noise threshold (turn-in).
glossary.at expert's apex (index N, phase k): The 'apex' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — argmax(|κ|) within the arc.
glossary.at expert's exit (index N, phase k): The 'exit' index of the k-th element of phase_indices.phases — arc offset where |κ| drops back below the noise threshold (track-out).

trajectory_offset.sentence_format_guide:
One paragraph per phase k from phase_indices.phases. Subject = player. Each paragraph has FOUR span beats in travel order — approach-to-entry, entry-to-apex, apex-to-exit, post-exit. Each beat picks ONE slope tag from {moving toward negative (tightening), aligned, moving toward positive (widening), recovery}: Approach-to-entry: "Player's line is across approach- to-entry of phase k (index → expert's entry (index )) — peak m at index ." Entry-to-apex: "Player's line is across entry- to-apex of phase k (expert's entry (index ) → apex (index )) — peak m at index ." Apex-to-exit: "Player's line is across apex-to- exit of phase k (expert's apex (index ) → exit (index

)) — peak m at index ." Post-exit: "Player's line is across post- exit of phase k (expert's exit (index

) → (index )) — peak m at index ." Final enumeration line — list EVERY beat (approach-to-entry, entry-to-apex, apex-to-exit, post-exit, every phase) whose tag is NOT "aligned". Skip only when every beat across all phases is "aligned".
common_description_errors: Do NOT default to 'aligned' when the trace clearly leaves the ±0.5 m band — exposing those deviations is the entire point of this graph

Refer to the trace as "the offset trace" or "the player offset", and to markers as "entry", "apex", and "exit" in the output — NEVER by their colour or shape (no "the green line", "the red star", "the yellow circle", etc.).  Colour and marker shape are only internal cues for identifying which marker is which
Read the SIGN — positive = player wider, negative = tighter — do not invert

Distinguish 'moving toward negative (tightening)' (offset growing more negative) from 'recovery' (trajectory offset shrinking toward zero from either side); a positive offset trending down toward zero is RECOVERY, not tightening
Distinguish 'moving toward positive (widening)' (offset growing more positive) from 'recovery'; a negative offset trending up toward zero is RECOVERY, not widening
For chicanes (multiple phases of opposite direction) the per-phase tagging convention holds — the underlying sign flip handles the direction switch, so positive still means wider in every phase
Anchor every callout to expert's entry / apex / exit indices from the phase_indices attachment
Do NOT interpret the offset as oversteer / understeer / over-limit — describe lateral offset only

cross_graph_guidelines.brake_and_speed: When both BRAKE and SPEED graphs are present: cross-reference the timing offsets.  If the player begins braking later than the expert by N indices AND begins decelerating later than the expert by ~N indices on the speed graph, state both offsets in the same sentence. Likewise check whether the index where each player trace's brake returns to zero aligns with the player's minimum speed index, and whether that alignment matches the expert's.  If the offsets differ by more than 3 indices, describe the gap explicitly.
cross_graph_guidelines.throttle_and_speed: When both THROTTLE and SPEED graphs are present: report the player's throttle-onset offset relative to the expert AND the player's acceleration-onset offset relative to the expert in the same sentence.  If there is a delay between the player's throttle application and the speed beginning to rise, describe it as "player has throttle applied for N indices before speed begins to rise" and state whether the expert shows the same delay.
cross_graph_guidelines.time_delta_and_features: When the TIME DELTA graph is present alongside brake, throttle, or speed graphs: describe which sections of the time delta line (gap to expert grows / shrinks / is stable) align with which index ranges on the feature graphs.  Example: "The gap to expert grows between indices 100–150, which corresponds to the braking zone where the player begins braking later than the expert by 10 indices and the player's peak brake pressure is lower than the expert's by 0.20."
cross_graph_guidelines.trajectory_and_features: When trajectory plots and feature plots are both present: describe which spatial sections of the trajectory (per-phase entry / apex / exit, or straight when phase_indices.phases is empty) correspond to which index ranges on the feature plots.  Anchor each statement to a (phase k, entry|apex|exit) where phases are present.  Example: "The brake graph's index 50–80 (where the player begins braking later than the expert by 8 indices) corresponds to phase 0's corner-entry section on the trajectory plot, where the player's entry line is wider than the expert's."
cross_graph_guidelines.balance_and_push_limit: When the SLIP BALANCE and PUSH_LIMIT graphs are both present: both are line plots indexed by segment index, so co-occurrence is a direct overlap of index ranges.  For every over-limit spike (identified by index range on the push_limit graph), check the balance graph at the same index range and report whether it sits above zero (oversteer / red), below zero (understeer / blue), or crosses zero (balance flip).  Phrase as "the over-limit spike across indices N–M co-occurs with an oversteer spike of peak X rad on the balance graph at indices N′–M′" — report co-occurrence without interpreting it.
cross_graph_guidelines.brake_and_throttle_overlap: When both BRAKE and THROTTLE graphs are present: describe any index range where the player has BOTH traces simultaneously above zero. Report start index, end index, the approximate values during the overlap, AND whether the expert shows the same overlap.  Phrase as "the player applies throttle and brake simultaneously between indices N–M (N indices); the expert shows no overlap / shows overlap of M indices".

trajectory_shape_vocabulary: Use these terms when describing the overall shape of a trajectory: - "Single curve": one continuous arc from Start to End. - "Straight": essentially a straight line with minimal curvature. - "Approach to corner": starts straight and begins curving toward the End marker. - "Exit corner leading to straight": starts curved and straightens out toward the End marker. - "S-shape / chicane": changes direction at least twice (left-right-left or right-left-right). - "Hairpin": a very tight, ~180-degree turn. - "Between consecutive corners": a brief transition between two corners — neither a full straight nor a full corner. - "Consecutive corners with no straight in between": multiple corners with no intervening straight (esses / chicane complex). Describe the shape first, then per-phase comparative details — iterate every entry in phase_indices.phases (each has its own entry / apex / exit).  Single-arc corners use phase 0; chicanes / esses cover all phases.
